skill.name: role-creator
skill.description: Create, install, and validate custom Codex agent roles with minimal role config files and optional safety controls.
skill.summary: Create Codex agent roles and register them in Codex configuration files.
skill.author: am-will

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many command, filesystem, environment, and weak-crypto patterns. Most are false positives from Markdown inline code, TOML examples, and legitimate local shell helpers; no malicious exfiltration or prompt injection evidence was found. The remaining risk is medium because the skill intentionally writes Codex config files and one helper interpolates MCP names into a tomlq expression.

medium_findings.title: Local Codex Configuration Mutation
medium_findings.description: The skill is designed to create role config files and update Codex config files, including the default home config path. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it can change agent behavior and should be disclosed before use.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly create directories, write TOML output, back up config files, and replace the target config. The behavior is intentional and local, not malicious.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled MCP Names Interpolated Into TOML Query
medium_findings.description: The role config writer builds a tomlq expression by inserting MCP server names into the filter string. A crafted name could alter generated TOML structure, although this is not direct shell execution.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The MCP name is trimmed and then concatenated into the expression rather than passed as a data argument. The impact appears limited to generated config manipulation.

low_findings.title: Optional Elevated Role Controls
low_findings.description: The script supports sandbox, network, and web search settings, including dangerous access modes. The skill instructions say these options should only be added after explicit user request, so this is a disclosure concern rather than confirmed abuse.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The elevated controls are clearly present, but the instructions repeatedly require explicit user request before applying optional settings.

low_findings.title: Static Documentation Patterns Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: Many static hits come from Markdown inline code, TOML snippets, placeholder paths, and schema references. The weak cryptography and Ruby backtick labels do not match executable cryptographic or Ruby behavior in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited areas are documentation and command examples, not hidden executable Ruby or cryptographic code. The examples align with the skill purpose.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Names Are Placeholders
low_findings.description: The environment access findings refer to MCP template examples that name required API key variables. No evidence found that the skill reads, stores, or sends secret values.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The files contain placeholder environment variable names for MCP configuration. There is no network request or secret-handling code tied to those placeholders.

dangerous_patterns.title: Config File Replacement
dangerous_patterns.description: The installer creates a backup and then replaces the target Codex config file with tomlq output. This is expected behavior, but users should review the target path before running it.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands explicitly copy the current config to a backup path, write a temporary file, and move it over the target path.

dangerous_patterns.title: Dynamic TOML Filter Construction
dangerous_patterns.description: The writer appends MCP server names into a tomlq filter string. This should be hardened by passing server names as data or validating a strict identifier pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The expression concatenation is visible and uses values derived from command line input. The likely impact is generated TOML manipulation rather than OS command execution.

content.value_statement: Custom agent roles are hard to configure safely because Codex config files have strict supported keys. This skill collects required inputs, writes role configs, registers them, and validates the result.
actual_capabilities: Collects required role inputs before writing files.
Generates minimal role config files with model, reasoning effort, and developer instructions.
Registers roles under Codex agent configuration entries.
Creates backups before updating the target config file.
Validates role declarations and role config keys against a schema.
Provides templates for restricted, minimal, full, and frontend-focused roles.
limitations: Requires local shell tools such as tomlq and jq for the helper scripts.
Does not validate every advanced config value beyond top-level schema keys.
Can modify Codex config files, so users must confirm target paths.
Advanced MCP server definitions may require manual template editing.

use_cases.title: Create a read-only research role
use_cases.description: Set up an agent role that investigates code and returns file references without editing files.

use_cases.title: Standardize team role setup
use_cases.description: Generate repeatable Codex role configs with consistent model, reasoning, and instructions.

use_cases.title: Validate custom role changes
use_cases.description: Check that a custom role uses supported declaration keys and includes required config fields.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic role
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner setup with required fields only.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Codex role named researcher. Use model gpt-5.3-codex, medium reasoning, and instructions to inspect code without editing files.

prompt_templates.title: Install in a project
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a project-scoped Codex role for this repository. Ask me for any missing role name, description, model, reasoning, and config path.

prompt_templates.title: Add restrictions
prompt_templates.scenario: Role with explicit sandbox and network limits.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a restricted reviewer role with workspace-write sandboxing, network access disabled, and clear instructions to report file evidence only.

prompt_templates.title: Build an advanced role template
prompt_templates.prompt: Draft an advanced Codex role for frontend architecture. Include only the optional controls I approve, then validate the final role config.

output_examples.input: Create a researcher role with medium reasoning and no file edits.
output_examples.output: The skill asks for missing required values, writes a minimal role config, installs the role, creates a backup, and reports validation status.
The skill checks the role declaration, confirms required fields, flags unsupported keys, and explains what must change.

output_examples.input: Show what advanced controls are possible.
output_examples.output: The skill summarizes sandboxing, web search, MCP, app connector, and feature options without applying them by default.

best_practices: Confirm role name, target config path, and install scope before running write commands.
Start with the minimal role template and add optional controls only when needed.
Run validation after every role update and review the backup path.
anti_patterns: Do not enable network access, web search, or broad sandbox permissions by default.
Do not overwrite an existing role unless the update is intentional.
Do not place unrelated settings under the role declaration entry.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates Codex role config files and registers them in a Codex config file.

faq.question: Does it modify my home config by default?
faq.answer: Yes. The default target is the Codex config under the user home directory unless a project config is selected.

faq.question: Can it create project-specific roles?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports project-scoped config paths when the user requests that scope.

faq.question: Does it enable network access automatically?
faq.answer: No. The instructions say optional controls such as network access should be added only after explicit user request.

faq.question: What tools are required?
faq.answer: The helper scripts expect standard shell tools plus tomlq and jq for TOML and JSON processing.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium warning because it writes local config files and needs careful path confirmation.
